summary: Every Slovak verb has an aspect: imperfective (ongoing/repeated) or perfective (completed/result)
Short base verbs without prefixes → usually imperfective (čítať, písať, robiť)
Adding a prefix → usually creates perfective (pre-čítať, na-písať, u-robiť)
Perfective 'present tense' form = future meaning (prečítam = I will read it through)
Suffixes -ávať / -ovať / -úvať → imperfective, even with prefix (odovzdávať, kupovať)
Aspect pairs: most verbs have an imperfective + perfective partner (písať ↔ napísať)
Some prefixes change meaning, not just aspect (písať → opísať = to describe, not 'write completely')
Past tense exists for BOTH aspects: čítal som (was reading) vs. prečítal som (finished reading)
Imperfective = used with ongoing, habitual, repeated, or background actions
Perfective = used with single completed actions, results, or sequential events
Motion verbs have special aspect pairs: ísť/chodiť, niesť/nosiť, viezť/voziť
When in doubt: imperfective for process, perfective for result

rules.title: Basic Concept
rules.text: Slovak verbs exist in aspect pairs. Imperfective (nedokonavé) verbs describe ongoing, repeated, or unfinished actions. Perfective (dokonavé) verbs describe completed actions with a clear result. Almost every verb belongs to one aspect — you must learn which.
examples: čítam knihu – I am reading a book (ongoing, imperfective)
prečítam knihu – I will read the book completely (perfective)
písal som list – I was writing a letter (past, imperfective)
napísal som list – I wrote/finished the letter (past, perfective)

rules.title: Short Verbs Are Usually Imperfective
rules.text: Short base verbs without prefixes are typically imperfective. They describe general, ongoing, or repeated actions and can be used in the present tense to describe what's happening now.
examples: čítať – to read (imperfective)
písať – to write (imperfective)
robiť – to do/make (imperfective)
jesť – to eat (imperfective)
piť – to drink (imperfective)
hovoriť – to speak (imperfective)

rules.title: Prefixes Create Perfective Verbs
rules.text: Adding a prefix to an imperfective verb usually makes it perfective. The prefix often adds a sense of completion or direction. The 'present tense' conjugation of a perfective verb refers to the future.
examples: čítať → prečítať – to read completely
písať → napísať – to write completely
robiť → urobiť – to finish doing
variť → uvariť – to finish cooking

rules.title: Prefixes That Change Meaning (Not Just Aspect)
rules.text: Not every prefix simply 'completes' the verb. Many prefixes change the meaning entirely, creating a new verb with its own aspect pair. Learn these as separate vocabulary, not as aspect partners.
examples: písať → napísať (write completely) — aspect pair
písať → opísať (describe) — NEW meaning, not aspect pair
ísť → prísť (arrive) — NEW meaning
robiť → vyrobiť (manufacture) — NEW meaning

rules.title: Suffixes -ávať / -ovať / -úvať = Imperfective
rules.text: Verbs with suffixes -ávať, -ovať, or -úvať are imperfective, even when they contain a prefix. These are often the imperfective partner of a prefixed perfective verb — they 're-imperfectivize' it for repeated or habitual actions.
examples: dávať – to give repeatedly (imperfective)
odovzdávať – to hand over repeatedly (imperfective)
kupovať – to buy regularly (imperfective)
otvárať – to open repeatedly (imperfective)

rules.title: Perfective Verbs Have No True Present Tense
rules.text: When you conjugate a perfective verb in 'present tense' forms, it actually means future. To talk about the present, you must use the imperfective partner.
examples: prečítam knihu – I will read the book (future, perfective)
čítam knihu – I am reading the book (present, imperfective)
napíšem list – I will write the letter (future, perfective)
píšem list – I am writing the letter (present, imperfective)

rules.title: Both Aspects in Past Tense
rules.text: Both imperfective and perfective verbs have past tense forms. The difference is whether the action was ongoing/repeated (imperfective) or completed with a result (perfective).

rows: I was reading / I used to read

examples: Čítal som knihu celý deň – I was reading a book all day (process)
Prečítal som knihu – I finished reading the book (result)
Varil som obed – I was cooking lunch (ongoing)
Uvaril som obed – I cooked lunch (it's done)

rules.title: When to Use Each Aspect
rules.text: Choosing the right aspect depends on what you want to express about the action.

rows: Action is ongoing: Čítam knihu (I'm reading)
Action is completed: Prečítal som knihu (I finished reading)
Action is repeated/habitual: Každý deň čítam (I read every day)
Action happened once with result: Včera som prečítal knihu (I read the book yesterday)
Describing background: Keď som čítal... (While I was reading...)
Describing a sequence: Prečítal som a potom som šiel spať (I finished reading and then went to sleep)
Duration/process matters: Čítal som hodinu (I read for an hour)
Result/completion matters: Prečítal som to za hodinu (I read it in an hour)
With 'still/already' ongoing: Ešte čítam (I'm still reading)
With 'already' done: Už som prečítal (I've already read it)

examples: Ongoing: Píšem email – I'm writing an email
Completed: Napísal som email – I wrote the email (done)
Habitual: Každý deň varím – I cook every day
One-time result: Uvaril som večeru – I cooked dinner (it's ready)

rules.title: Common Aspect Pairs to Memorize
rules.text: The most important aspect pairs. The imperfective is listed first, perfective second.
examples: kupovať / kúpiť – Kupujem auto (I'm buying) vs. Kúpil som auto (I bought it)
dávať / dať – Dávam ti to (I'm giving you) vs. Dal som ti to (I gave you)
brať / vziať – Beriem si to (I'm taking it) vs. Vzal som si to (I took it)
hľadať / nájsť – Hľadám kľúče (I'm looking for keys) vs. Našiel som kľúče (I found keys)

rules.title: Irregular Aspect Pairs
rules.text: Some aspect pairs use completely different roots rather than prefix/suffix changes. These must be memorized as separate vocabulary items.
examples: hovoriť / povedať – completely different words
brať / vziať – completely different words
hľadať / nájsť – imperfective = searching, perfective = finding

rules.title: Motion Verbs — Special Aspect System
rules.text: Motion verbs have a unique system: unidirectional (one-way, specific) vs. multidirectional (habitual, general). Both are imperfective. Adding a prefix creates the perfective.
examples: Idem do školy – I'm going to school (right now, one direction)
Chodím do školy – I go to school (regularly/habitually)
Prišiel som do školy – I arrived at school (perfective, completed)
Nesiem tašku – I'm carrying a bag (right now)
Nosím okuliare – I wear glasses (habitually)

rules.title: Aspect with Negation
rules.text: Negation works with both aspects but the meaning differs. Imperfective negation = didn't do it at all / wasn't doing it. Perfective negation = didn't manage to complete it.
examples: Nečítal som knihu – I wasn't reading the book / I didn't read (at all)
Neprečítal som knihu – I didn't finish reading the book
Nepísal som – I wasn't writing
Nenapísal som – I didn't manage to write (it)
Nevaril som – I wasn't cooking
Neuvaril som – I didn't manage to cook (it)

rules.title: Quick Decision Guide
rules.text: Use this to quickly decide which aspect to use.

rows: Is the action happening right now?
Is it a habit or repeated action?
Am I describing a process or duration?
Is the action completed with a result?
Did it happen once and it's done?
Am I listing a sequence of completed events?
Am I talking about future completion?
Am I using 'every day / always / often'?
